title: Lectures 6 7 Questions without answers
body:
Lectures 6 & 7


Which of the following statements is false about portfolio management?

A. Portfolio management process involves 3 big steps.
B. Execution refers primarily to portfolio construction and the determination of asset allocation.
C. Among other things, the Investment Policy defines the investment objectives, and the
constraints to be met.
D. The optimal asset mix is not very sensitive to the long-term capital market assumptions.


Which of the following statements is false about forecasting using regressions?

A. Predictive regressions are similar in spirit to factor models for returns except that the
explanatory variables are lagged relative to the variable to be predicted whereas in return factor
models all variables are contemporaneously measured.
B. The HAR model for volatility is practical as it can be simply estimated using OLS regression
unlike the GARCH model.
C. Often the signals used to forecast returns are different that the one used to forecast variance
or volatility.
D. Forecasting expected returns out-of-sample is simpler than in-sample.


Which of the following statements is true about forecasting market returns using regressions?

A. There is some evidence that inflation, percentage of equity issuing, term-spread, and VIX-
squared have some predictive power for market returns out-of-sample.
B. The out-of-sample R-squared obtained for a given signal is often higher than the in-sample R-
squared.
C. Constructing out-of-sample forecasts requires knowledge of the future as it is done
consistently with the way an investor would implement a model to trade on it.
D. Forecasting expected returns out-of-sample is simpler than forecasting volatility.

Python exercise: Before you attempt the exercise below, review Application 2 – HAR model and
familiarize yourself with the Python code. Once you understand better the code, save the code
with another name, for instance, Recommended Exercise – HAR model.ipynb. The exercise
below will require you to modify certain parts of the code.

Instructions:
    • Load mkt daily excess returns from the pickle "Lecture6_7.pkl" and start your sample
        from 2004/10/15
    • Construct 5 measures of realized variances that you will express in annual units.
            o 2D RV_t: The realized variance recorded as of time t computed using returns on
              day t-1 and t.
           o 4D RV_t: The realized variance recorded as of time t computed using returns on
              day t-3, t-2, t-1, and t.
           o 6D RV_t: The realized variance recorded as of time t computed using returns on
              day t-5, t-4, t-3, t-2, t-1, and t.
           o 6D RV_t+1: The realized variance recorded as of time t computed using returns on
              day t+1, t+2, t+3, t+4, t+5, and t+6.
   •   Estimate the following HAR model:
         6D RV_t+1 = alpha + beta_1 * 2D RV_t + beta_2 * 4D RV_t + beta_3 * 6D RV_t + u_t+1

Question: What is the R-squared of the HAR model?




Which of the following statements is true about LTCMAs?

A. It is somewhat easier to derive LTCMA forecasts for corporate bond returns as it only involves
forecasting 2 terms once we assume that the average corporate bond trades at par value while
LTCMA for stock portfolios requires to forecast 3 components.
B. If we expect substantial default of firms going forward the bond LTCMA will be higher all
things being equal.
C. We can use the same modeling assumptions to derive LTCMA forecast for illiquid asset classes
as the ones used for corporate bonds and stocks.
D. When constructing an asset mix, LTCMAs for a large class of assets cannot be freely obtained
from third party providers.


Which of the following is not a key input to portfolio construction in the mean-variance (MV)
framework?

A. Expected returns on assets
B. Expected volatilities of assets
C. Covariance between assets
D. The expected probability of negative return of the assets


What is the primary objective of an Investment Policy Statement (IPS) in the portfolio
management process?

A. To outline general rules for the portfolio manager
B. To describe the performance objectives and risk tolerance
 C. To protect investors from unethical behavior
D. All of the above



In predictive regression used for forecasting future returns, the lagged variable in the regression
model:

A. Is always equal to the current return
B. Represents a variable observed or constructed one period before the variable to be predicted.
C. Predicts next year’s return with certainty
D. Is typically random and does not affect future returns



Which of the following is true about the predictive power of variables for equity returns as found
by Goyal and Welch (2008)?

A. Predicting equity returns is generally easy
B. Only a few variables are robust predictors out-of-sample
C. All variables used in predictive regression have high statistical significance
D. In-sample performance is irrelevant when evaluating model quality



When constructing an optimal portfolio using mean-variance analysis, which of the following
constraints is typically not considered?

A. The sum of weights equals 100%
B. A maximum weight invested in bonds
C. A fixed portfolio return of 25%
D. A minimum weight for each asset



In forecasting volatilities using the GARCH(1,1) model, which of the following statements is
correct?

A. GARCH assumes volatility is constant over time
B. Volatility clustering and persistence are features of GARCH models
C. GARCH models predict returns, not volatility
D. GARCH cannot be used to forecast future periods
 You are running a predictive regression to forecast S&P 500 monthly excess returns. The
regression model is 𝑟𝑟𝑡𝑡+1= α+ β 𝑑𝑑𝑡𝑡 + u𝑡𝑡+1 where 𝑟𝑟𝑡𝑡+1 is the S&P 500 excess return in month t+1,
𝑑𝑑𝑡𝑡 is the predictive variable (e.g., dividend yield) observed in month t. You estimate the model
using monthly data from January 31st, 2015, to October 31st, 2024. You obtain α = 0.005 and β =
0.9. If the dividend yield was 0.02 at the end of September 2024 and was 0.04 at the end of
October 2024, what is the predicted S&P 500 excess return for November?

A. 0.037
B. 0.041
C. 0.0415
D. 0.045




You consider a GARCH(1,1) model used to estimate S&P 500 excess return volatility. Using 10
years historical data on daily S&P 500 excess returns, you estimate the following parameters:
𝜎𝜎 2 = 0.0010 , 𝛼𝛼 = 0.16 , and β=0.64. On Monday 14th, 2024 the conditional GARCH variance
was 0.002 and the S&P 500 daily excess return was 0.01%. Given the information above, what is
the conditional variance forecast for S&P 500 daily excess return on Tuesday 15th, 2024? For this
question you can assume that the S&P 500 expected daily excess return (𝜇𝜇) is 0.

A. 0.001480
B. 0.001698
C. 0.001324
D. 0.000746


You estimated a GRACH model on monthly excess returns of a portfolio. You got the following
GARCH(1,1) model parameters:
- 𝜎𝜎 2 = 0.0075
- 𝛼𝛼 = 0.10
- β = 0.70
- 𝜇𝜇 = 0.01 (monthly portfolio expected return)
- The most recent volatility estimate: 𝜎𝜎𝑡𝑡2 = 0.0085
- The previous month portfolio’s return: 𝑟𝑟𝑡𝑡 = 0.01
                   𝑗𝑗−1
- ∑12𝑗𝑗=1 (𝛼𝛼 + β)      = 4.656403

Using the GARCH(1,1) model, what is the one-year ahead volatility forecast?
 A. 30.01%
B. 23.45%
C. 29.96%
D. 26.56%
______________________________________________________________________________

You are using the HAR model to predict the 1-day ahead volatility of an asset based on the daily
(D), weekly (W), and monthly (M) realized volatilities. The HAR model is given as:

                                𝑑𝑑
                           𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑡𝑡+1 = α + β𝑑𝑑 𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑡𝑡𝑑𝑑 + β𝑤𝑤 𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑡𝑡𝑤𝑤 + β𝑚𝑚 𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑡𝑡𝑚𝑚 + u𝑡𝑡+1
with
- α = 0.005
- β𝑑𝑑 = 0.4, β𝑤𝑤 = 0.3, and β𝑚𝑚 = 0.3
- Realized volatility for the last day 𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑡𝑡𝑑𝑑 = 0.02
- Realized volatility for the last week (average of the last 5 days) 𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑡𝑡𝑤𝑤 = 0.015
- Realized volatility for the last month (average of the last 22 days) 𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑅𝑡𝑡𝑚𝑚 = 0.017

What is the forecasted 1-day ahead variance by HAR model?

A. 0.0161
B. 0.0187
C. 0.0202
D. 0.0226

______________________________________________________________________________


Python exercise: Before you attempt the exercise below, review Application 3 – GARCH model
and familiarize yourself with the Python code. Once you understand better the code, save the
code with another name, for instance, Recommended Exercise – GARCH model.ipynb. The
exercise below will require you to modify certain parts of the code.

Instructions:
    • Load NVDIA daily excess returns from the pickle "Lecture6_7.pkl" and start your sample
        from 2004/10/15
    • Estimate the following GARCH model:
                                         r𝑡𝑡 = 𝜇𝜇 + 𝜎𝜎𝑡𝑡 u𝑡𝑡 with u𝑡𝑡 ~N(0,1)
                         𝜎𝜎𝑡𝑡 = 𝜎𝜎 + 𝛼𝛼 ( ( 𝑟𝑟𝑡𝑡−1 -𝜇𝜇)2 − 𝜎𝜎 2 ) + β (𝜎𝜎𝑡𝑡−1
                            2     2                                       2
                                                                              − 𝜎𝜎 2 )
    • For this, you will initialize 𝛼𝛼 and β to 0.15 and 0.70, respectively

Question: What is the 𝛼𝛼 estimated?
______________________________________________________________________________
 Python exercise: Before you attempt the exercise below, review Application 4 – Mean-variance
Allocation and familiarize yourself with the Python code. Once you understand better the code,
save the code with another name, for instance, Recommended Exercise – Asset allocation.ipynb.
The exercise below will require you to modify certain parts of the code.

Instructions:
    • Download in python the monthly returns stored in “Assets_returns_Lectures 6 & 7.xlsx”
    • From all the assets considered, drop the illiquid assets: Infrastructure, RealEstate, and
        PrivateEquity.
    • Estimate annual expected returns and covariance using the average annualized returns
        and the annualized variance-covariance matrix.
    • Find the optimal portfolio invested in the remaining assets. For this apply the following
        constraints:
            o Target return = 9%
            o Bounds for each asset: (minimum weight, maximum weight):
                    (0.02, 0.4), # UniverseBond - Gov Bonds
                    (0.02, 0.4), # CorporateBond - Corpo Bonds
                    (0.02, 0.3), # Canada - Canadian Equities
                    (0.02, 0.3), # USLarge - US Large Cap Equities
                    (0.02, 0.3), # USSmall - US Small Cap Equities
                    (0.02, 0.3), # MSCI - World Equities
                    (0.02, 0.15) # Emerging - Emerging Equities
    • Asset type level bounds:
            o Sum of weights in bonds (UniverseBond + CorporateBond) <= 40%
            o Sum of weights in equities (Canada + USLarge + USSmall + MSCI+Emerging <= 60%
    • Sum of all weights = 1.


Question: What are the optimal weights?
